refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation link availability ≥ 0.999 (99.9%) over the agreed evaluation period, measured as the ratio of time the bidirectional command-and-telemetry link is operational to total evaluation time.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-010 addresses a critical operational requirement: remote equipment control depends on reliable communication. The refined FR quantifies the acceptance criterion (≥0.999 availability), specifies the measurement scope (bidirectional link), and defines the evaluation window. This is essential for safety-critical teleoperation where link loss can lead to equipment immobilization or unsafe states.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Continuous monitoring of link state (up/down) during evaluation period; calculate availability as (total_time_link_up) / (total_evaluation_time). Acceptance: measured availability ≥ customer-defined target (default 0.999). Failure modes: network outage, transceiver failure, antenna misalignment, interference.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-010.1: The teleoperation system shall establish and maintain a bidirectional command link from tele-operator to vehicle.
FR-NGHE-010.2: The teleoperation system shall establish and maintain a bidirectional telemetry link from vehicle to tele-operator.
FR-NGHE-010.3: The teleoperation system shall detect link loss and trigger safe state transition within defined timeout.
FR-NGHE-010.4: The teleoperation system shall provide redundancy or recovery mechanisms to minimize link downtime.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement dual-path wireless communication (primary + backup network) with automatic failover; monitor link health continuously; trigger safe shutdown on persistent loss.
DP-B: Implement single high-reliability wireless link with error correction and retransmission; rely on network provider SLA for availability.
DP-C: Implement wired tether-based command/telemetry with wireless backup; prioritize wired path for availability.
DP-D: Implement mesh network topology with multiple relay nodes to provide path redundancy and self-healing routing.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-A: Implement dual-path wireless communication (primary + backup network) with automatic failover; monitor link health continuously; trigger safe shutdown on persistent loss.

potential_sources: DP-A couples to FR-NGHE-010.3 (safe state transition) and FR-NGHE-010.4 (redundancy) directly; no hidden coupling to unrelated FRs.
Failover logic couples to Safety Services (SafetyAlert interface) for safe shutdown coordination.
Link monitoring couples to Telemetry interface for health status reporting.
coupling_check.notes: DP-A is well-decoupled from vehicle propulsion, steering, and payload control FRs. It is orthogonal to equipment-specific operational modes. Coupling is intentional and traceable to sub-FRs.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Two independent wireless networks (e.g., LTE + private 5G or satellite) are available at the worksite.
Assumption: Failover switching can occur within acceptable latency (< 100 ms) without loss of critical commands.
Risk: Dual-path infrastructure cost may exceed budget; customer may not have access to two independent networks.
Risk: Failover logic complexity increases software testing burden and potential for race conditions.
Risk: Link health monitoring overhead (CPU, power) may impact vehicle battery life on extended missions.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-A to specify: (1) primary/backup network selection criteria (e.g., signal strength, latency, packet loss); (2) failover timeout threshold (e.g., 3 consecutive missed heartbeats); (3) safe state definition (e.g., brake engaged, engine idle); (4) monitoring interval (e.g., 100 ms heartbeat). Add fallback to DP-B (single high-reliability link) if dual-path infrastructure unavailable.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Owns the dual-path wireless communication stack, link health monitoring, failover logic, and safe state transition triggers. Interfaces with Vehicle for command injection and telemetry collection; interfaces with Safety Services for safe shutdown coordination. Does NOT own physical network infrastructure (carrier networks, antennas) or vehicle propulsion/steering control.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives operator commands (steering, throttle, auxiliary controls) from tele-operator console; routed through primary or backup network path.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Transmits vehicle state (position, orientation, sensor readings, link health status) from vehicle to tele-operator console; routed through primary or backup network path.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Signals link loss or degradation events to Safety Services; triggers safe state transition (brake, engine shutdown) if link unavailable for > threshold duration.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Abstracts the underlying dual-path wireless infrastructure (primary + backup networks); Teleoperation module manages path selection, failover, and health monitoring at this interface.

module_containment_scores.notes: Teleoperation is the natural owner of link availability logic. Dual-path failover, health monitoring, and safe state triggers are core teleoperation concerns. Minimal diffusion: only SafetyAlert coupling to Safety Services (expected and necessary).

Vehicle module owns propulsion and steering; link availability is not a vehicle-specific concern. Assigning DP-A to Vehicle would scatter communication logic across vehicle firmware, creating high diffusion and poor cohesion.

Safety Services owns safe state policies and shutdown coordination, but NOT the communication infrastructure or failover logic. Assigning DP-A to Safety Services would conflate network management with safety policy, violating separation of concerns.
NGHE Solution is the system-level container; assigning DP-A here would bypass architectural layering and prevent reuse of Teleoperation module across different NGHE variants.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Teleoperation module is the optimal owner because: (1) it encapsulates all communication path logic (primary/backup selection, failover, health monitoring); (2) it owns the Tasking and Telemetry interfaces; (3) it minimizes diffusion by centralizing link availability concerns; (4) it maintains cohesion with other teleoperation functions (command encoding, latency compensation, operator feedback).
containment_risks: Risk: If Vehicle module also implements link monitoring (e.g., for autonomous fallback), logic duplication and inconsistency may occur. Mitigation: Vehicle queries link status via Teleoperation interface; does not implement independent monitoring.

Risk: If Safety Services implements its own failover logic (e.g., independent timeout), race conditions may occur. Mitigation: SafetyAlert is unidirectional (Teleoperation → Safety Services); Safety Services reacts to alerts, does not drive failover.

Risk: If network infrastructure (antennas, carrier contracts) is managed outside Teleoperation, interface contracts must be explicit. Mitigation: Network Link interface abstracts infrastructure; Teleoperation assumes dual-path availability per interface SLA.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Clarify interface contracts: (1) Network Link interface must specify dual-path availability SLA (e.g., each path ≥ 0.99 availability independently); (2) SafetyAlert must specify safe state transition latency budget (e.g., ≤ 500 ms from link loss detection to brake engagement); (3) Telemetry interface must include link health metadata (signal strength, latency, packet loss rate) for operator awareness.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-A (dual-path wireless with failover) directly addresses FR-NGHE-010 (link availability ≥ 0.999). Redundancy and failover are the primary mechanisms to achieve high availability. DP-A is traceable to all sub-FRs (010.1–010.4). No logical gaps or misalignment detected.

diffusion_risks: Low diffusion risk: Teleoperation module is the natural owner; failover logic is cohesive with command/telemetry routing.
Moderate coupling risk: SafetyAlert interface couples Teleoperation to Safety Services. Mitigation: interface is unidirectional and event-driven; no circular dependency.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation achieves 0.95 containment score. All DP-A logic (dual-path selection, health monitoring, failover) fits within Teleoperation scope. External interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are well-defined and necessary. No artificial splitting or duplication required.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification is feasible: (1) Continuous link state monitoring during evaluation period; (2) Automated failover testing in lab and field; (3) Availability calculation via telemetry logs. Acceptance criterion (≥0.999) is measurable and objective.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: Teleoperation module owns link availability; Vehicle and Safety Services own complementary concerns (propulsion, safe state). Responsibilities are non-overlapping and traceable.

recommended_revisions: Refine DP-A to specify failover timeout threshold (e.g., 3 heartbeat misses = ~300 ms at 100 ms interval) and safe state definition (e.g., brake engaged, engine idle, no steering input).
Define Network Link interface SLA: each path must provide ≥ 0.99 availability independently; Teleoperation achieves ≥ 0.999 via redundancy.

Add verification plan: (1) lab failover testing (inject link loss, measure recovery time); (2) field availability monitoring (log link state every 100 ms); (3) statistical analysis (calculate uptime ratio over evaluation period).
Clarify safe state transition latency budget: SafetyAlert must trigger brake engagement within ≤ 500 ms of link loss detection.

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-010 is not atomic; it decomposes into four sub-FRs (010.1–010.4) that require independent design and verification. DP-A is sound and well-allocated to Teleoperation, but sub-FRs must be refined further to address: (1) command link establishment and maintenance (010.1); (2) telemetry link establishment and maintenance (010.2); (3) link loss detection and safe state transition (010.3); (4) redundancy and recovery mechanisms (010.4). Each sub-FR will have its own DP and IM assignment at the next level.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-010.1: The teleoperation system shall establish and maintain a bidirectional command link from tele-operator to vehicle with latency ≤ 200 ms and packet loss ≤ 1%.
FR-NGHE-010.2: The teleoperation system shall establish and maintain a bidirectional telemetry link from vehicle to tele-operator with latency ≤ 500 ms and packet loss ≤ 5%.
FR-NGHE-010.3: The teleoperation system shall detect link loss (no heartbeat for > 300 ms) and trigger safe state transition (brake engagement, engine idle) within ≤ 500 ms.
FR-NGHE-010.4: The teleoperation system shall implement automatic failover from primary to backup network path within ≤ 100 ms and restore primary path when available.

assumptions: Two independent wireless networks (e.g., LTE + private 5G) are available at the worksite.
Failover switching latency (< 100 ms) is acceptable for teleoperation safety.
Link health monitoring overhead (CPU, power) is within vehicle power budget.
Safe state (brake engaged, engine idle) is sufficient to prevent hazards during link loss.
Operator is trained to recognize link degradation and take manual control if needed.
open_risks: Dual-path infrastructure cost may exceed budget; customer may not have access to two independent networks.
Failover logic complexity increases software testing burden and potential for race conditions.
Link health monitoring overhead may impact vehicle battery life on extended missions.
Failover timeout threshold (3 heartbeats = ~300 ms) may be too aggressive or too lenient depending on worksite conditions.
Safe state definition (brake, engine idle) may not be sufficient for all hazard scenarios (e.g., equipment on slope).
evidence_needed: Network availability data for candidate wireless providers at representative worksites.
Failover latency measurements from prototype dual-path implementation.
Vehicle power budget analysis including link health monitoring overhead.
Safety analysis (FMEA, HAZOP) to validate safe state definition and timeout thresholds.
Field trial data: measured link availability over ≥ 30-day evaluation period at customer site.
